draft.introduction: Proposition 25 of Proclus’s Elements of Theology establishes the triad of remaining, proceeding, and reverting (mone, proodos, epistrophe) that structures all of reality. Every effect remains in its cause, proceeds from it, and reverts toward it.

translation.text: Whatever is complete proceeds to the production of those things which it is capable of producing, imitating the one originative principle of all. For that principle, because of its goodness, provides being to all things. So too every complete being, by virtue of its completeness, generates things posterior to itself. Each thing desires to produce offspring, and each thing at its best makes something and advances toward the generation of the next.
translation.original_text: πᾶν τὸ τέλειον εἰς τὴν τῶν δευτέρων πρόοδον πρόεισιν

translator_notes.note: The principle that perfection naturally produces—that goodness is self-diffusive (bonum est diffusivum sui, as the Scholastics would say)—bridges Neoplatonic emanation and Christian creation theology. Pseudo-Dionysius adopted this Proclean principle to explain why God creates: not from need but from the overflow of goodness.
